speaker: interviewer
text: The interviewer. We're in Los Angeles. We're speaking English. So tell me the-- finish the story about the rabbi.

speaker: interviewee
text: About the Rabbi-- so he say to the people what he thinks, you know. And he say, I'm going to Israel to buy a house, or rent a tenement with my older son. His name was Abraham. I used to play with him soccer in my yard. And when I will have everything ready in Israel, I will come  in, I will take the rest of my family with to Israel. He left. And in this time, the Russians come over. The Russians come over, you cannot go out. From Russia, nobody comes out, you know. The only thing can come out is a pigeon or a diplomat. If you're not a pigeon, and not a diplomat, you have to stay there. [LAUGHS] She was there. She couldn't go out. And after a year, the Germans come in. Then they killed all the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're going to get to that right now. Let's start talking about, let's talk about your family. You're now-- in 1940, you would have been 25 years old. Right? You were born in 1915. So in 1940, 25. So you were working-- what were you doing? What kind of work were you doing?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Russians come over?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Russians come over, my factory was in Panevezys. 50 miles from Panevezys was a city, Siauliai. So we had a branch there. When the Russian come over, I, I was staying there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Siauliai?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Siauliai. And in Siauliai, when they-- they made a special law when [? they count ?] us. When they took over after the war, the Russian, they didn't make the same, like in Russia, all the rules. They go step by step. They didn't bother too much. Like in Russia, you couldn't have a church or a synagogue. In Lithuania, they didn't touch. They could have the synagogues, they could have their religion. And they made a law you can have a shop with three people working for you. In Russia, you cannot work even for yourself. Everything is the government. But in Lithuania, there is a lot of shops-- small little shops. When they would ruin this, they would destroy the country, you know. They don't do this. Everything was good. They make a little bit different. And they made this law, I opened up a shop to make brushes. I used to make brushes in my house. And I didn't have to work for somebody else. So I was out of control from the-- they call this a union, you know. Even the union is nothing worth it in-- what they call this, a union. And I survived. I was living like this, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your family's company?

speaker: interviewee
text: Family's company? When the, when the Russians come over, they nationalized right away, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you, you lost everything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No question about it. Beside [INAUDIBLE], this is [INAUDIBLE] will take maybe 20 years to tell you, when the Russian took over this. To the [NON-ENGLISH] was in Panevezys. He was the production manager. They didn't let him out. Even they took over everything. They didn't let him out. He had to stay and teach people to take this over.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he had a house in the factory's yard, you know? He had to go out because they was afraid. He was the owner. Maybe he can do something to the factory. They don't tell us this yet. He had to go out in a different place. He had to go to work. And I wasn't there. So you were in a smaller town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I wasn't a small town. This town, where I come over, is bigger like Panevezys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, it's as big as Panevezys?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. A little bit bigger. But the only thing is, I wasn't there. When they come over in Panevezys, when I would be-- I wouldn't be here now. In Panevezys, when the chairman come over, they took all the Jewish people, they surround them in one place. And a week later, they took them out in the middle of the city, I think. I got this from a museum. I got the history from Israel, a museum. They digged out big places, you know, I think twelve graves. And they shoot them, you know, kill them, and they cover them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were people from your family there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was my sister, with her husband, with two children. [INAUDIBLE] maybe for my brother's mother when her sister was there. Maybe more, but I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your own mother and father? Where were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father and mother died before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they die?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother die in 1936. She died in a city, we had a store there. It's a German city. It used to belong to Lithuania, but used to be a German autonomy. The chairman was run by Germans, you know. This belonged to Lithuania, the city. But all the rules, and the courts, and the police-- everything is German, because the population are German. And we had a license from the, from this government. And we opened up a store there. And we was there. My father was there, and my father's mother, and her brother, and I was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of that place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same Belski-- oh, Memel. Memel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Memel was the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And in Lithuanian, Klaipeda.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, OK. So, and your father was gone, too, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my father was there. But the only thing is, she died in '36. And they buried her in a cemetery in this city. See? And after this, when Hitler took over-- after their arrival, Hitler took over Memel. Because it's a German city, he just called up the Lithuanian government, and they couldn't do nothing, and they took it over.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we left for Panevezys, you know. My father became sick, you know. And they took him to Austria. They operate on him. He come over back, nice, healthy. And a week later, he became sick again. Took him to a Jewish hospital. Took a few hours, and he died. He die in Kovno. In this time, Kovno was the capital. So You were living in-- say the name of the town, again. Siauliai?

speaker: interviewee
text: Siauliai.

speaker: interviewer
text: Siauliai. Did you have your own family yet? Were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I wasn't married. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So then--

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother-- I  had a brother, who I showed the picture. He is in Israel. He married in Shavel, there. I was staying with him, you know. And the youngest brother, he was in Memel. He was going in a college for business administration. When they took over Memel, he come over in Siauliai, you know. He couldn't go back in college because when the Russian took over, and he was like a son from a capitalist. The capitalists they didn't let go in, in college.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was working for the government in a, in a candy factory. He was-- he had a job. He had to calculate how much cost a pound candy to make, you know. He had a government job. So now when you started your own little brush company, did your brother that, that lived there, that you lived with, did he work with you, too, in the brush company? Or what did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had, he had a store, a branch. They still didn't, they didn't have time to nationalize. And he was running this store till they nationalized.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. OK. Now, how was it for the Jews in the town, Siauliai? How was it-- were Jews-- what year are we talking about, first of all?

speaker: interviewee
text: You're talking when the Russian come over?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, when you were in Siauliai.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was with the Russian.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1940 was, you know, there was a lot of business people that took away the business from them. They had manufacturer, they took away the manufacture place. And they had-- for the rich people, they made tough, you know. But the poor people, the workers, they was working the same like they [INAUDIBLE]. The only thing, we had are factory there. A Jewish man, Frankl, had a leather factory there. And I think by 4,000 Jewish people was working there. And because of this, the people in Siauliai survived. Because of this. Because otherwise, they would kill the Jewish people in Siauliai, the same like in Panevezys. But because of this factory-- and I wasn't working there. See, but the only thing I was in this time in Siauliai, they made a ghetto for these people, because they didn't want to close up the factory. Because they wouldn't have expert trade people who can run the factory. They made a special ghetto, and they didn't touch-- they killed a lot of people, you know, older people, and invalids, you know. But the only thing I was there between them. And most of them worked in this factory. But we had dates, you know. This and this date, they going to kill us, like in Panevezys, like in the other cities. But they, they call us a commissioner from, from German government. He was the, the rulers on this. When he had an order they taking away the Jews to kill, he used to run away to Berlin. And he used to come in from Berlin with an order to stop it. Because he used to say, if you kill the Jewish people, we have to close the factory. They don't want to close the factory. It's very important for the German army to have this factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it leather factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: The leather factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they using the leather for the military?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Must be, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at this time that you're describing to us, this is when the Russians and the Germans were still allied, and they were jointly running--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in this time is already there was war. They was allied for one year. And after a year, the German started war with Russia. There was no allied already. The Russian was already enemies from Germany. And Germany was-- they took over this before the Russian government had the factory-- Frankl's factory. When he by himself, Frankl was in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now we're talking about 1941? Because you say, now, that the Germans are running--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: When in 1941? Was it spring, summer?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they took over Lithuania, I think was in, in summertime, I think in May or June.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that time, did you know-- and did your family know, and your friends-- that what was happening to Jews in other parts of Eastern Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard, even from Panevezys. We didn't know right away. But after this, we fine-- we used to hear. But we couldn't have connection, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: No radios or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No radios. No papers. I don't know if we could have even a pen or pencil. We was in ghetto, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now let's talk about the ghetto. Were you living in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was living in the ghetto, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the ghetto surrounded? Was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: With police-- the Lithuanian police.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lithuanian police. Did it have barbed wire around it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Barbed wires, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you allowed to go out of the ghetto at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to allow to go just to work. You see? They used to take people factories used to call up. And they used to send them out to work. Or the military-- the German military. I was out to work in my little shop. We worked for the-- the city took over. A Jew couldn't have it-- the city, when I was managing with my brother, you know. But I used to take out from the ghetto 60 people to go to work every day to the shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you still making brushes, or back to rope?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we made rope, too, because my brother come over from Panevezys, where they was holding him in the factory. One was a small little shop from rope in a Jewish man had. He come over before the German come in. And he worked with him, you know. Because I was allowed to have a little shop in the Russian's atmosphere, you know. But after this, when the German come in, we used to make in the same place, we used to make the-- because the city put a-- the brush shop was in my house. And I couldn't live there. They took out this, and they, they give a place, you know. And there's a bigger place, they used to make rope, too. Not in big scale, but we used to make it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people lived in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto maybe was-- there was two  halves of the ghetto. They not in one place. Over the street was another part, near the cemetery. And one was near the Frankl's shop, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which part were you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was near the Frankl shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live by yourself, or with a lot of--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, with a lot of people. The only thing is, before they established the ghetto, it's a Christian. But they told him he had to go out because they're making a ghetto. Till he come over in Siauliai and he took over the house where I was living with our family. And we was over in his house. We had, I think, the best place in the ghetto. Even the management, the president from the ghetto-- because the German made-- they don't talk to all the Jews. They make a few people, and they talk to these few people. And he was like a president and like a leader. They want even to take away from me the, the place. I don't know if he was fighting on he could hold on. But the only thing is, a lot of people. We was living maybe 18, 20 people in a room.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a room?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a room.

speaker: interviewer
text: How'd you get the food? What kind of food?

speaker: interviewee
text: The food was very bad, you know. They used to give according to the ration, you know. They used to let in so much. You can get so many people. They used to send in so much food, you know. Even the Germans, they had calories. They have to have so many calories-- 1500. But the only thing, I had better, because I used to go out to work, and I was in connection with Lithuanian people, Christian, who used to work in the shop. So I used to get food a little bit more, like anybody else. But the only thing, to bring in the ghetto is very hard. Because they used to look, like you go now in the airport.

speaker: interviewer
text: They'd search you.

speaker: interviewee
text: They search you. If they find something, it's trouble. If they find a pack of cigarettes, they hanged one man. They find a pack of cigarettes. You couldn't do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You couldn't have cigarettes in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: You could do it if they don't find it, you know. But to bring in, in the ghetto, you cannot bring in nothing. And this was Lithuanian police, you know. They was-- before the war, they was acting like friends, brothers, you know. No brothers, but friends, you know. But when the German come in, before they had the orders, they start to kill the Jews on their own. And even the German-- when the army comes in, a German army comes in, they go, and they fight, and they come in our city. In Shavel, they come in, in Siauliai. It was a field commandant. He come in, and he was ruling for a few days. After this he goes, again. He's go on the way to Russia, you know. But in the few days where he says there, before he come in, the Lithuanian people started to go in the Jewish places and kill them, and take all the stuff, and they used to drag the bundles. Till he find out, he didn't like it. He send out the German army to stop this. But when a few days later when he goes out, then the SS comes in. Then they come in and they started to rule us already with the Jewish population, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: The people that you were living with in the ghetto, were these, besides your family, were they strangers to you? Before-- I mean, obviously.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Some were stranger, but we know each another. Even I wasn't too long a citizen of this city, you know. But the same, I know a lot of people there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have money-- ghetto money-- special printed money?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think we had-- we couldn't have money at all. It's not allowed to have money. Because in the ghetto, they took away-- they put tables, you know. And if somebody brought in money, you know, like they had dollars, or gold, or silver, you know, they put tables once in a while, and they say, put on the table everything you got. If you don't put it, they'll find it, will kill you on the spot. So people put everything, you know. But yeah, everything. We were official. We had marks, you know, the German marks. We had connections sometime. The brother where we had now, he took very high risks, you know. The younger brother-- who is now in Israel-- he was in Los Angeles. He used to go out as a Christian, without the stars. And he used to bring connection with Lithuanian people, you know. He used to sell, sometime, a watch. He was in a airport with my brother. He used to go out even sometime and buy products from the farmers. We had a good commandant, you know, from the German commandant. He was a fisherman by trade, an old man. But he was good to the Jewish people. He used to, he used to buy us to send out a truck, and used to bring in this stuff for the people. They should have a little bit better food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this for the ghetto, he would do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. This is-- from the ghetto, they took out when the Lithuanian and Germans used to control. But after this, after two years, I think, the SS come over. And they took over the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had no staff, you know. They used to go 500 people each day from the ghetto to work on the airfield near Shavel, there-- a mile from Shavel. They say, no more. He build barracks there, and they have to stay there. When they have to stay there-- I didn't work there, in this time. But the only thing is, the president from the ghetto decided older people and married people not to send them. Just single and young one. I and my brother they send out there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So then, now you were moved out to the barracks to work on the airfield?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and I worked there. But I didn't work in-- like the other people, heavy work, you know. I worked-- they had a brush shop. And I made the brushes for the airport, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- so how long were you in the ghetto before they moved you?

speaker: interviewee
text: A couple of years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So like 1943, now, we're talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And after this, they put us, maybe, for eight months or the year, I  was in this camp, you know, in the barracks from the airport.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the airport? Or what was the name of the area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Siauliai.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's still Siauliai.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they had a name for the airport, like we got here. [INAUDIBLE]. I don't remember the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it like a military airport?

speaker: interviewee
text: A military. Military. This military, not civil. They used to come in, we had pilots there from Italy, you know, all the allies from Germany. They used to take off with the planes and bomb. We used to load-- the people from the Jewish ghetto, they used to load the bombs, you know, in the planes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you were doing the brushes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your brother one of the people that they sent out to the barracks? Or just you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Me and my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: The youngest one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So now in the barracks, can you describe that for us? Can you describe the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: The barracks was-- you see, the commandant, he was nice to us. And after this, when the SS took over the ghetto, we had old people and kids-- 900. So one day, he come over with police, I think-- Lithuanian police, or Ukrainian police, or military. And they put them on buses, you know, the kids. That they took out my sister's two kids, and I think my brother's kids, and 800 more. They took they out and they destroyed them, must be.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling me a story before, when we did the questionnaire, that they said to somebody, if you don't like where they're going-- do you want to see where they're going to take--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me that story again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. When they took out the kids, they come over with nurses, you know, nice German nurses. They got uniforms, and they got-- they're nice looking, too, the German girls. [LAUGHS] They put the kids on buses, you know. And when he say, the commandant-- the SS commandant, he was running this-- he say, you cannot take care of the kids in the ghetto. We will take them in a place where they will have good, supervised, you know, and good food, and clothes, you know. Because it's no good for kids to-- The president from the ghetto-- I forgot his name. [NON-ENGLISH], I think. I forgot his name. His son survived, I think, and he's now in Israel. Then he say, I know where you take them. Then he say, if you don't believe us, we will take him. You go with him. And after this, you will come in back and you will tell the ghetto members, your people, where you left them. So I wait-- still I'm waiting, now, to see him. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So the president never returned?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. Never. Never. There was a lot of stories what happened, you know. Even these SS men, and the police chief from the Lithuanian police. We had a doctor. He's now in New York. When after the war, he was a doctor for the United Nations, for the Jewish. They made a special hospital for the survivors, you know. He was the doctor in ghetto. In the ghetto, they have a little place, like two by four. And some bad cases, they used to take in for a few days, you know. So one time, a lady ran away from a place where they kill the Jewish people. Not even from Lithuania. She was going place by place, till she find out it's a ghetto. And she was in the ghetto-- the Siauliai ghetto. Until they find out. The commandant find out. He made an hour, he's going to shoot her, you know, because she doesn't belong here. But he was the doctor from this SS man, he had a ulcer. The Jewish doctor was good, you know, for him, because he helped him. The doctor say to him, look, if you will make this, the whole ghetto will get like a revolution, you know. You cannot do this. For me, do me  a favor. I will tell you something to do. I will take her in hospital, he say. They got a little place, you know, where he is there. And I will give her a needle, and I will kill her. And then we'll take her out. Nobody will know nothing. He agree with him, because it's his doctor. And what he did, instead to take her out, somebody died natural. So he took her the other one. And she is alive. Or he is alive. I think when after the war, they was united, where he had saved them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that's a good story. OK. We're going to stop the tape.
